_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.2: This is just informational text showing the name of a health consultation hotline service called 'Nayami Kyukyu Zero' - no action needed from you.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.3: This is the actual phone number (0120-783-990) for the 24-hour health consultation hotline - save this number in case you need medical advice.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.5: This bullet point describes one service offered - calling when you have concerning symptoms or health worries that you want to discuss with medical professionals.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.6: This bullet point explains another service - getting advice about daily life precautions regarding exercise, diet, and other health-related lifestyle matters.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.9: These are contact numbers for the Health Checkup and Health Promotion department - use these for questions about preventive health services and regular checkups.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.10: This is the official Itami City website URL where you can find more information about city services and health programs online.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.12: This is just a notice that the document was printed using vegetable oil-based ink for environmental reasons - no action required from you.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.1: This is a section header explaining the National Health Insurance system structure - no action needed, it's informational content about how the system works.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.2: This is a section header about how insurance tax rates are determined - no input required, it's explanatory content about the calculation methods used.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.5: This section header lists benefits available through National Health Insurance - no writing needed, it's informational content about coverage types.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.8: This indicates administrative/management operations - likely refers to how the system is operated, no input required from applicants.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.10: This explains benefit #5 for transportation costs when medical transport was necessary - no action needed, it's describing a covered benefit type.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.12: This describes funds allocated for insurance benefit expenses - informational text about how the system finances benefits, no input required.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.13: This explains high-cost medical expense rules for people under 70 - informational content about age-based benefit calculations, no writing needed.
_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.14: This describes the payment process through the National Health Insurance Federation - explains the administrative payment system, no input required.

_vision_explanations:Page 2 - Section 1:.2: This refers to a system that provides financial relief when combined medical and long-term care costs exceed certain thresholds within a household. Foreign residents should be aware this benefit exists and may apply for reimbursement if their combined annual medical and care expenses are high - consult your local municipal office for eligibility and application procedures.

_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.12: This is a reference note, not a field to fill out. It directs you to page 17 for information about the lump-sum birth and childcare allowance (出産育児一時金) - no action needed here.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.11: This shows '2割' (20%) which is the reduced premium rate that applies when your household income falls within the lowest tier of the income-based reduction system. This is an automatic calculation - you don't need to write anything here, but it indicates your premium will be reduced to 20% of the standard amount if your income qualifies for this category.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.2: Check this box or mark 申請 (application) to indicate you are applying for the tax reduction. This is typically required to be selected if you want to claim the deduction.
_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 1:.5: This is explanatory text stating that eligible preschool children will receive reductions as shown in the table above. No action needed - this is just informational text explaining the policy.

_vision_explanations:Page 5 - Section 2:.1: This is informational text, not a field to fill out. It explains that preschool children (未就学児) who qualify under certain conditions will receive tax reductions as shown in the table above.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.3: This shows the payment schedule for the following year (columns for January, February, March of the next year). No action needed - this is just informational showing when future payments are due.
_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.8: These are digital payment options available for paying insurance premiums. You can use any of these mobile payment services (Mobile Regi, PayPay, d Payment, auPAY, Rakuten Pay, or J-Coin Pay) by scanning the barcode on your payment slip with the respective app.

_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.1: This shows the food expense amounts you'll pay per meal during hospitalization, which varies based on your income level and tax status. You don't need to write anything here - these are reference amounts the hospital will charge you.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.2: This indicates that the food expense amounts shown are calculated per individual meal. You don't fill this in - it's just explaining how the costs are measured.

_vision_explanations:Page 9 - Section 1:.1: This section is for requesting reimbursement when you paid full medical costs upfront and want the national health insurance to reimburse their portion later. You'll need to submit receipts and proof of treatment along with your insurance card to get back the amount exceeding your normal copayment.
_vision_explanations:Page 9 - Section 1:.4: Use this when you received medical treatment while traveling abroad (not for medical tourism) and want partial reimbursement from Japanese national health insurance. You'll need to submit Japanese translations of all foreign medical documents, receipts, your passport showing travel dates, and a consent form for overseas medical expense investigation.

_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 1.2: This indicates that health checkups and comprehensive medical examinations (人間ドック) are not covered by national health insurance. You cannot receive insurance benefits for routine health screenings or preventive medical checkups.
_vision_explanations:Page 10 - Section 1.3: This shows that home nursing care medical expenses are covered by national health insurance when a doctor determines you need medical care at home. You only pay a portion of the cost, and the national health insurance covers the remainder.

_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.4: This shows the calculation to determine total medical expenses from the co-payment amount. Divide your actual co-payment (90,000 yen) by the co-payment rate (10%) to get total medical costs.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.9: This calculates your self-payment limit based on income bracket. Use the formula: base amount + (total costs - threshold) × 1% to determine maximum out-of-pocket expense.
_vision_explanations:Page 11 - Section 1.15: This shows your benefit amount - the difference between what you paid and your legal limit. Subtract your calculated self-payment limit from your actual payment to get the refund amount.

_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.1: This section applies when you have household members both under 70 and between 70-75 years old - you'll need to calculate benefits for both age groups separately then combine them.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.3: Refer to the calculation table on page 22 of this form to determine the specific benefit amounts for the 70-75 age group based on their income level.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.5: This shows the threshold amount (842,000 yen) for high-income earners - if total medical expenses exceed this, you pay 1% of the excess plus the base amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.6: Enter 140,100 yen as the monthly limit for high-income earners (income over 6.9 million yen), plus 1% of expenses exceeding 842,000 yen.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.9: Add any amount over 21,000 yen from the under-70 group to this limit amount, then use the page 22 table to calculate the final household benefit.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.10: This is the expense threshold (558,000 yen) for middle-income earners - expenses above this amount are calculated at 1% plus the base limit.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.11: Enter 93,000 yen as the monthly limit for middle-income earners (income 3.8-6.9 million yen), plus 1% of expenses over 558,000 yen.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.12: Enter 80,100 yen - this is the base monthly limit for standard income earners, with 1% added for expenses exceeding 267,000 yen.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.13: Enter 44,400 yen - this is the monthly limit for low-income earners and applies as a flat rate regardless of total medical expenses.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.14: Important calculation rules: each month runs from the 1st to the last day, and you must calculate separately for each medical institution.
_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.15: The annual cap is 44,400 yen for low-income households, with a maximum limit of 57,600 yen per year for certain categories.

_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 1.1: This shows the monthly premium amounts for Low-Income Group II health insurance - 8,000 yen for individual coverage and 24,600 yen for family coverage. This is a reference table showing costs, not a field to fill out - it helps you understand what you'll pay monthly if you qualify for this low-income insurance category.

_vision_explanations:Page 12 - Section 2.6: This shows '6月' (June) as part of an example timeline demonstrating how medical cost limits change when turning 75 years old. This is informational text showing that in June, after the 75th birthday in May, the person would return to the higher limit of 57,600 yen under the elderly medical system.

_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.1: This is informational text directing you to check Itami City's homepage for the latest updates about health screenings - no action needed here.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.4: This describes that a doctor will perform a physical examination including listening with a stethoscope - this is part of the screening process, not something you fill out.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.5: This explains that blood pressure measurements (both systolic and diastolic) will be taken to check for hypertension - this is a test performed during screening, not a form field.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.6: This describes blood tests that will check for diabetes, cholesterol problems, and kidney/liver function - these are medical tests included in the screening, not information you provide.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.8: This explains that urine samples will be collected to test for sugar and protein levels to check kidney and urinary tract health - this is a medical test, not something to fill out.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.10: This indicates group health screenings are available but require advance reservations and have limited spots - contact them early to secure your preferred date.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.11: Check the city's official newsletter or website for specific dates and times when group screenings are available - these change regularly so verify current schedules.
_vision_explanations:Page 16 - Section 1.12: This points to where you can apply for group screenings, likely through a QR code or link - use this if you prefer group screenings over individual appointments.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This header indicates the section is for the applicant's information. No action needed - it's just a label.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.5: This is pre-printed text addressing the mayor - leave as is. It shows which municipal office will process your application.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.9: Enter your phone number in the format shown (XXX-XXX-XXXX). Use hyphens to separate the area code and number segments.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.10: Write your full name as it appears on your residence card. For foreign residents, use the same romanization or kanji that matches your official documents.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.14: Enter your Individual Number (マイナンバー) - the 12-digit number from your My Number card. Write it clearly with spaces between every 4 digits as shown.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.5: Check this box if the head of household is the same person as the applicant filling out the form. This saves you from having to write duplicate information.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.6: Enter your My Number (マイナンバー), the 12-digit individual identification number issued to all residents of Japan. You can find this on your My Number card or notification card.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.7: Write your primary contact phone number where officials can reach you during business hours. Include the area code and use hyphens to separate the number segments as shown.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.8: Enter the name of the city, town, or ward where you currently live. Write this in Japanese characters (hiragana, katakana, or kanji) as it appears on your residence registration.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.9: Write the detailed address of the head of household, including apartment/building name and room number if applicable. Use the same format as your official residence registration (住民票).

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.2: Check this box if the contact person is the same as the applicant filling out the form. This saves you from having to write duplicate information in the contact fields.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.3: Enter your 12-digit My Number (個人番号) without spaces or hyphens. This is the unique identification number issued to all residents of Japan.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.4: Write your primary phone number where you can be reached regarding this application. Use the format shown with hyphens between area code and number.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.5: Write the name of your city, town, or municipality where you live. For foreign residents, this should match your resident registration (住民票).
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.6: Write the complete address of the head of household or primary contact person. If you checked 'same as applicant' above, this would be your own address.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.8: This appears to be a continuation or clarification field related to the head of household information above. Fill in any additional details about the household head if required.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.1: Write the full names of all family members who will be listed on this residence certificate, starting with the head of household. Names should be written in the same format as registered in your official documents (typically kanji/katakana for foreign residents).

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.8: This shows an example entry for the second household member named 'Kakogawa Hanako'. You would replace this with the actual name of your second family member in katakana/hiragana if they are foreign, or kanji if Japanese.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.1: Check this box if you are the head of household (the main person listed on the household registration). This is typically the primary resident or family head whose name appears first on official documents.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.13: Check this box if you are between 70-74 years old. This age bracket may qualify you for special healthcare benefits or reduced premiums under Japan's medical insurance system.

8b435bc3c5ae8ddb0c51550dde7b4e67.note: Enter the date when your residence registration should begin (usually your move-in date)

85e786687a3d87757d4f7a291ce55a17.note: Leave blank if you don't have Japanese health insurance, or enter your insurance period dates if you do

128f27ad33cfdb8f7148454cea6195d4.note: Check this box if registering a grandchild in relation to the household head

67984825c30bfc373fdb5248faf3345a.note: Check this box if you do not have a usage registration for municipal services

f00a4c3e4750dd017f23c44c71dec00a.note: Enter the name of your workplace or affiliated business if applicable, leave blank if not employed or self-employed

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This indicates the form is for applicant use only - no need to fill anything here as it's just a header label.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.3: Write your complete residential address in Japan, including postal code, prefecture, city, and apartment/building details if applicable.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.4: This is pre-printed addressing the form to the Mayor of Kakogawa City, Hyogo Prefecture - leave this as is since it's the destination authority.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.6: Enter your phone number using the standard Japanese format with hyphens separating the area code and number segments.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.7: Write your full legal name as it appears on your residence card or other official Japanese documents, using the same script (katakana for foreign names).

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.7: This section appears to be for recording changes in employment status, residence, or life events after moving. Leave blank if you haven't experienced job changes, moved residences, or had major life status changes since your initial registration.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.8: Check this box if the head of household's address is the same as the applicant's address, which is common for most applications.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.9: Leave this field blank (indicated by the dashes) - it appears to be a placeholder or separator field that doesn't require input.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.10: Write the name of your city in Japanese characters. In this example 'Kakogawa City' is shown - replace with your actual city name.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.11: Write the complete address of the head of household in Japanese, including prefecture, city, district, block numbers, and apartment information if applicable.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.4: Check this box if the applicant's address is the same as the head of household's address, which is common for family applications.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.5: Write the postal code in the format XXX-XXXX (3 digits, hyphen, 4 digits) for the head of household's address.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.6: Write the city name where the head of household lives - in this example it shows 'Kakogawa City'.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.7: This is a label indicating 'Address of the head of household' - you would continue writing the full address below this section.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.10: This continues the address label from field 7, indicating this entire section is for the head of household's address information.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.6: Check this box if you are the head of household applying for yourself. This is the most common selection for individual applicants.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.9: Check this box if you want documents to be delivered by mail rather than picked up in person. This is convenient for foreign residents who may have difficulty visiting government offices.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.12: Fill in additional required information as specified by the form context. Common entries include phone numbers, alternative contact information, or specific details about your situation.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.3: Check this box if you are between 70-74 years old and have a senior citizen medical certificate. This relates to Japan's elderly healthcare system where those 70-74 receive reduced medical costs with proper certification.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.8: Write your postal code in the format shown (3 digits, dash, 4 digits). You can find your postal code by searching your address on Japan Post's website or asking at your local post office.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.1: Check the box for tax office confirmation tax return filing, which is the most common purpose for requesting tax payment confirmation documents.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.2: Check this box if you're submitting to the tax office for your tax return - this is typically what most people need.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.3: Leave unchecked unless you specifically need to submit to city hall for resident tax purposes.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.4: Leave unchecked unless your company specifically requires this for year-end tax adjustment procedures.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.5: Leave unchecked unless you need this document for financing procedures with banks or other financial institutions.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.6: Check this box if you're submitting to a court - typically for legal proceedings or visa applications.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.7: Check this box if you're submitting to lawyers, administrative scriveners, or other legal professionals for immigration or legal matters.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.8: Use this space to specify any other submission destination not listed above, such as immigration office or embassy.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.9: Check the free tax payment confirmation document option - this is what most people need for tax returns and visa applications.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.10: Write the first date you want covered (usually January 1st of the tax year you need). Fill in year, month, and day in the format shown.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.11: Write additional date ranges if you need multiple periods covered, following the same year/month/day format as above.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.12: Read this note carefully - if you need a paid certificate (300 yen), confirm with the submission destination whether the free confirmation document is sufficient.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.13: This is just an informational note explaining that once you register for home delivery, you don't need to register again for future requests.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.14: Leave unchecked unless you specifically want the document mailed to you in January for tax return purposes.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.15: Check this box only if you want automatic annual mailing of your tax payment confirmation document every January.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.3: Check this box if you want to receive a QR code version of your document or certificate. This is useful for digital verification and storage on your smartphone.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.4: This appears to be a processing section for staff use to track document workflow (reception, fax, creation, delivery). Leave this blank as it will be filled by the municipal office staff.

_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.1: This is a reference note indicating that various types of income should be included (such as salary, business income, irregular income, miscellaneous income, and public pension benefits). No action is required as this is explanatory text marked as reference material.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.2: Enter the total amount of income from forestry operations. If you don't have forestry income, leave this blank or enter 0.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.3: Enter income amounts that are calculated separately from other income, such as capital gains from real estate sales under separate taxation, or securities income that you filed separately. Only include amounts you specifically chose to file under separate taxation.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.4: This shows the basic deduction amount of 430,000 yen that applies when your previous year's total income was 24 million yen or less. No entry needed - this is automatically applied.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.5: This shows the basic deduction amount of 290,000 yen for income between 24-24.5 million yen. The system will automatically select the correct amount based on your income level.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.6: This shows the basic deduction amount of 150,000 yen for income between 24.5-25 million yen. The appropriate amount is automatically determined by your total income.
_vision_explanations:Page 3 - Section 1:.7: This indicates 0 yen basic deduction for income over 25 million yen. The system automatically applies the correct deduction amount based on your income bracket.

_vision_explanations:Page 6 - Section 1:.1: This section shows the maximum assessment limits for different types of insurance premiums. These are predetermined amounts set by the government - you don't need to fill anything in here, it's just informational showing the caps for medical care (65万円), elderly support (24万円), and nursing care (17万円) insurance portions.

_vision_explanations:Page 7 - Section 1:.4: This is an automatically calculated subtotal for the Late-Stage Elderly Support Payment section (116,807 yen). You don't need to write anything here as it's the sum of the three amounts above it.
_vision_explanations:Page 7 - Section 1:.5: This shows the income-based calculation for Long-term Care Insurance (1,730,000 yen × 0.0256 rate = 44,288 yen). The system calculates this automatically based on your reported income.
_vision_explanations:Page 7 - Section 1:.6: This is the subtotal for Long-term Care Insurance premiums (63,072 yen). It's automatically calculated by adding the income-based amount and per-person amount above.
_vision_explanations:Page 7 - Section 1:.8: This shows the final calculation for your total annual insurance premiums by adding all three insurance categories together (361,920 + 116,807 + 63,072 = 541,799 yen). This total determines your monthly payment amount.

_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.5: This is an informational note stating that the underlined portions in the table above only apply when calculating for 2 or more salary earners. No action needed - just read and understand this applies only to households with multiple income earners.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.6: This is a reference note explaining who qualifies as a 'salary earner' based on three income conditions listed below. No writing required - this is just clarifying the definition used in the calculations above.
_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 1:.11: This is an informational statement about tax law limitations regarding special deductions for capital gains income. No action needed - it's just explaining that certain tax deductions don't apply when using this income reduction system.

_vision_explanations:Page 8 - Section 2:.3: This is explanatory text that doesn't require any input from you. It's explaining that when calculating income deductions for insurance premiums, the income of the deemed household head is not included in the standard income amount, but it is included when determining premium reduction eligibility.

_vision_explanations:Page 9 - Section 1:.3: This is informational text stating who is eligible for the maternity/paternity national health insurance premium reduction - specifically those scheduled to give birth (or who have given birth) on or after November 1, 2023. No input is required in this field as it's just explaining the eligibility criteria.

_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.4: Enter '100%' if your income decreased by exactly 100% (complete loss of income). This applies when you lost all employment or business income due to circumstances like job loss or business closure.
_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.5: Enter '100%' if your income amount was reduced by exactly 100% from the previous year. This corresponds to a complete elimination of your taxable income.
_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.6: Select this range if your income decreased by 90% or more but less than 100%. This means you retained some small amount of income but lost nearly all of it.
_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 1.7: Select this range if your income decreased by 30% or more but less than 40%. This represents a moderate reduction in income that still qualifies for some tax reduction benefits.

_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 2.1: This indicates a tax reduction rate of 30% or more but less than 40% for eligible individuals. If your income has decreased within this range compared to the previous year, you may qualify for this level of reduction.
_vision_explanations:Page 13 - Section 2.4: This explains exclusions from the tax reduction program. You are not eligible if your income reduction comes from investment gains/losses, stock dividends, or one-time income, or if you've secured new employment with company insurance or your income has already recovered.

_vision_explanations:Page 14 - Section 1.2: This is an informational statement explaining that reduction determinations will be made including the number of household members and their income. No action is required from you - this is just explaining how the system works for calculating potential reductions in national health insurance premiums based on household size and total household income.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.11: These bullet points are for listing additional family members or dependents. Write the names and basic information of other household members who should be included in this registration.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.3: These bullet points are likely for listing family members or dependents. Write the names of your spouse, children, or other family members who live with you, one per bullet point.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.4: Continue listing additional family members or dependents here if you have more than what fits in field 3. Leave blank if you don't have additional entries.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.5: Use this space for any remaining family members or dependents that didn't fit in the previous bullet points. If you have no additional entries, leave this blank.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.12: These bullet points appear to be for listing insurance or medical coverage details. Write the names of your health insurance plans or medical coverage types, one per bullet point.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.13: Continue listing additional insurance or medical coverage information here if you have multiple types. Include any supplementary insurance or coverage plans you may have.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 4:.14: Use this final bullet point for any remaining insurance or medical coverage that didn't fit above. Leave blank if you have no additional coverage to list.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.7: These bullet points are for listing additional insurance types or coverage details for the male family member in row 3. Leave blank if only using the standard checkboxes, or write specific insurance plan names/numbers if applicable.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.8: These bullet points are for listing additional insurance types or coverage details for the male family member in row 4. Use this space for any supplementary insurance information that doesn't fit the standard checkbox categories.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.9: These bullet points are for listing additional insurance types or coverage details for the male family member in row 5. Fill in only if you have specific insurance details beyond what the pre-printed checkboxes cover.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.3: These are bullet points for listing additional healthcare coverage options. You would check the boxes and fill in details for any supplementary health insurance you have beyond the main national health insurance.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.4: These bullet points are for specifying the frequency of medical visits or treatments. Select the appropriate option (daily, weekly, monthly, etc.) that matches your healthcare needs or current treatment schedule.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.5: These appear to be bullet points for additional medical information or special circumstances. Fill in any relevant details about your health condition, ongoing treatments, or other medical factors that may affect your healthcare coverage.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.2: Check the appropriate boxes if you've moved from another municipality or abroad and need to discuss income adjustments or payment arrangements. If unsure, leave blank or consult the tax office.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.5: Enter today's date in Japanese format (year/month/day). Staff may fill this field, so you can leave it blank if submitting in person.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: This field appears to be for a country or region code. As a foreign resident, you would likely write your country of origin or residence status designation here.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.8: Write the name of your city of residence. If you live in Takatsuki City as shown in the example, write '高槻市'. Otherwise, write the name of your actual city of residence in Japanese characters.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.11: Leave this field blank with dashes (ー) as shown, since this appears to be for official use only or not applicable to most residents.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 2:.12: Leave this field blank with dashes (ー) as shown, since this appears to be for official use only or not applicable to most residents.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.5: Enter the date you want to submit this form or have it processed. Use the Japanese date format with era year (令和/平成), month, and day.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 3:.7: Enter your date of birth using the Japanese calendar system. You'll need to convert your birth year to the appropriate Japanese era (Showa/Heisei/Reiwa) - online converters are available to help with this conversion.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.1: Write the phonetic reading (katakana) of your name in the furigana field above where you'll write your actual name. This helps officials pronounce your name correctly and is required on most Japanese forms.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 5:.4: Check this box if you are moving into this municipality from another city/town in Japan or from overseas. This is the most common option for foreign residents establishing residency in a new area.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.2: Check this box if you are moving into this municipality from another city/town in Japan or from overseas. This is the most common option for foreign residents establishing residency in a new area.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.1: Check this box if you want the tax office to calculate an estimate of your tax amount based on the information you provide. This is helpful for planning purposes but doesn't commit you to paying that amount.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.2: Check this box if you are the head of household (taxpayer) filling out your own tax return. Most individual taxpayers should check this box unless someone else is filing on your behalf.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 6:.6: Check 'Moving in' if you moved to this municipality from another city in Japan or from abroad during the tax year. This affects how your local taxes are calculated and which municipality has jurisdiction.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.2: Check this box if you are submitting a trial calculation or estimate rather than a final tax return. This is typically used when you want to see how much tax you might owe before filing your actual return.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.3: Check this box if you are the head of household (本人) filing this form for yourself. As a foreign resident, you would typically check this if you are filing your own tax documents rather than having someone else file on your behalf.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 7:.7: Check the appropriate box if you moved to this municipality during the tax year - '転入' if you moved from another place in Japan, or '非自発' if you moved involuntarily (such as due to job transfer). Leave unchecked if you lived in the same municipality all year.

_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.1: Write '(国民健康保険用)' which means 'For National Health Insurance' - this indicates the form type and should already be printed on the form.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.2: Write your health insurance number (被保険者番号) as shown on your National Health Insurance card.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.3: Write your family register number (宛名番号) which can be found on official city documents or your insurance card.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.4: Leave this blank - this is for the receiving office stamp (受付印).
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.5: This is the form title 'Application for Cancellation of My Number Card Health Insurance Benefit Registration' - should be pre-printed.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.6: This shows 'To the Mayor of Toyonaka City' and should be pre-printed on the form - no action needed.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.7: Write today's date in Japanese format: year (年), month (月), day (日). Use Japanese era year (like Reiwa 5年) or Western year.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.8: Write your family name (surname) in the フリガナ row using katakana characters.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.9: Write 'year of birth' (生年) - this should be filled with your birth year in Japanese format.
_vision_explanations:Page 1 - Section 1:.10: Write your given name (first name) in the second フリガナ section using katakana characters.
